title: Chickpea Salad
tagline: A fresh, 10-minute no-cook chickpea salad, boosted with soya chunks for gym-day protein.
macroNotes: Estimated from ingredients, including the soya chunks.
Most of the fat is olive oil — 2-3 tbsp is 240-360 kcal, so measure it. Use 1.5 tbsp to bring a serving down to ~430 kcal.
Protein is high (~40 g/serving) thanks to the soya chunks. Without soya it's ~14 g/serving — in that case add feta or top with a boiled egg or two for the protein.
Naturally very high fibre (~15 g/serving); drain and rinse the chickpeas well to keep it easy on the gut.

items: 100 g dry soya chunks (50 g per serving), boiled and chopped
1-2 tomatoes, diced (or a handful of cherry tomatoes, halved)
A handful of fresh parsley or cilantro, chopped

steps.title: Prep the soya
steps.body: Boil 100 g dry soya chunks for 4-5 minutes, drain, and squeeze them dry hard (squeezing matters — waterlogged soya dilutes the dressing and tastes bland). Chop into small bite-sized pieces.

steps.title: Combine
steps.body: In a bowl, combine the 400 g rinsed chickpeas, the chopped soya, 1 diced cucumber, 1-2 diced tomatoes, 1/4 finely chopped red onion and a handful of chopped parsley or cilantro.

steps.title: Dress it
steps.body: Drizzle over 2-3 tbsp olive oil and the juice of 1 lemon. Season with salt and black pepper, and chilli flakes if you want heat.

steps.title: Toss and rest
steps.body: Toss well and let it sit 5 minutes so the flavours blend and the soya soaks up the dressing.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.body: Top with feta or diced avocado before serving, if using.

notes: Meal-prep friendly: keeps in the fridge 3-4 days. If prepping ahead, hold the avocado and add it fresh so it doesn't brown.
Squeezing the boiled soya dry is the one step that actually matters here — do it properly.
Skip the soya if you'd rather keep it a light Mediterranean side; then lean on feta or a boiled egg for protein.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
